A Progress Update on the ARE (ArachnoScript Runtime Environment)
Hey everyone,
I’ve been deep in the weeds with the ArachnoScript Runtime Environment (ARE) and wanted to share a key design decision I just finalized regarding parameter passing.
In an earlier Medium post, I mentioned that ArachnoScript (AS) parameters would be deep-copied by default, with an explicit specifier for shared references. After further implementation work and performance considerations, I’ve reversed that default.
New approach:

• Parameters are shared by default (pass-by-reference semantics, similar to JavaScript).

• Use a dedicated specifier when you want a deep copy for safety.
Why the change?

• Better performance — Avoiding unnecessary deep copies on every function call significantly improves speed, especially in hot code paths.
- Familiar developer experience — Aligns more closely with JavaScript behavior that most devs already expect.
- Safety when needed — You still get explicit control to create copies and prevent accidental argument mutations, giving you the best of both worlds.
This adjustment strikes a stronger balance between ergonomics, performance, and safety — core goals for ArachnoScript.
I’m excited about how this is coming together in v0.2.5 and beyond. The runtime continues to evolve rapidly with the new parser, WASM support, FFI, and other improvements.
What are your thoughts on parameter passing defaults in a JS-inspired language? Do you prefer explicit copies for safety, or performance-first with opt-in protection? Drop your feedback below — it genuinely helps shape the language.
Thanks for following the journey! Try the latest ARE build and let me know what you build with it.
